nodes.title: Gender & articles: le, la, un, une

explanation.body: Every French noun is either le or la. That article is where French stores gender, so learn the two as one unit.

examples.english: the coffee, the street — gender learned automatically

explanation.body: Le and la both collapse to l' before a vowel sound. Les and des cover all plurals regardless of gender.
bullets: le / la — the (singular): le métro, la fille
l' — the (before a vowel): l'eau, l'école
un / une — a (singular): un café, une table
les — the (plural); des — a/some (plural)

explanation.body: Two rules cover thousands of words. Exceptions exist — collect them as you meet them.
bullets: -tion, -sion, -té → la: la situation, la solution, la liberté
-age, -ment, -eau → le: le message, le moment, le gâteau

explanation.body: A wrong article almost never blocks understanding. Get it right where the ending tells you, guess everywhere else, and keep talking.

examples.english: wrong gender — you still get a large coffee

explanation.body: Fast French drops ne and swallows syllables, but the article stays glued to its noun. It's your signal that a noun just went by.

examples.french: T'as vu la fille qui parle français ?

nodes.title: Plurals & adjective agreement

explanation.body: Written French adds -s to nouns, but that -s is silent. The article does the work: le becomes les, un becomes des.

examples.english: I want some friends. (amis sounds exactly like ami)

explanation.body: Des is the plural of un/une. Drop it and the sentence sounds clipped and wrong.

examples.french: On va manger des petites choses.
examples.english: We're going to eat some small things.

explanation.body: Adjectives match gender and number: petit, petite, petits, petites. The plural -s on petits is as silent as the one on cafés.
Some masculine adjectives end in a silent consonant. Adding feminine -e wakes that consonant up.
bullets: petit → petite — the t sounds
bon → bonne — the n sounds; vowel shifts too
blanc → blanche — the c becomes /ʃ/

examples.french: C'est pas pareil, la table blanche et les tables blanches.
examples.english: It's not the same, the white table and the white tables.

explanation.header: Train your ear on the article
explanation.body: Noun endings won't signal plural in speech — the article always will. Catching le vs. les and un vs. des is your fastest cue.

nodes.title: Adjective placement: before or after?

explanation.body: Put the adjective after the noun. That's the reverse of English, and it's right the vast majority of the time.

examples.english: Do you have a difficult question?

explanation.body: These nine go before the noun, always. They're the most frequent adjectives in the language, so they become automatic fast.
bullets: bon / bonne — good
petit / petite — small
grand / grande — big, great
gros / grosse — big, fat
beau / belle — beautiful
jeune — young
vieux / vieille — old
nouveau / nouvelle — new
mauvais / mauvaise — bad

explanation.body: The adjective sits between the article and the noun every time.
A handful of before-club adjectives change meaning by side. Context usually makes it clear.
Spoken French uses gros, petit, and vrai to punch up a noun — not to describe size, but degree. Think of them like 'total' or 'real' in English.

nodes.title: Prepositions: à, de, en, au, chez

explanation.header: à and de: the core pair
explanation.body: à marks direction or location. de marks origin or belonging.

examples.english: It's the dish of the day. (belonging)

explanation.header: Mandatory contractions with le and les
explanation.body: à and de never sit directly before le or les — French fuses them, no exceptions.
bullets: à + le → au: au café, au bar
à + les → aux: aux toilettes
de + le → du: le plat du jour
de + les → des: la fin des vacances
à la and de la stay unchanged — only le and les fuse

explanation.body: Countries have grammatical gender; cities don't. Use en before feminine countries (most of them), au before masculine countries, and à before any city.

examples.french: Elle est en France, à Paris.
Il vit au Japon, à Tokyo.
examples.english: He lives in Japan, in Tokyo.

explanation.body: chez means 'at someone's place or practice' — anywhere defined by a person, not a building. It always takes a person or pronoun, never a thing.

examples.french: On doit aller chez le médecin.
examples.english: We have to go to the doctor's.

explanation.body: au, du, aux, chez never drop in fast speech — they stay intact. What contracts is everything around them: je → j', tu → t', nous → on.

examples.english: Did you see the restaurant's menu?

nodes.title: Question forms: the full toolkit

explanation.body: French has three question forms. You'll produce two; you'll only recognise the third.
bullets: Intonation — spoken default, no word order change
Est-ce que — neutral, works anywhere
Inversion (viens-tu ?) — formal/written; recognise it, don't produce it

explanation.body: Keep statement word order. Raise your voice at the end. That's it.

explanation.header: Est-ce que: safe in any situation
explanation.body: Put est-ce que before a normal statement and you have a question. Use it with strangers, at work, or when intonation alone feels unclear.
These pair with est-ce que or the street pattern below.
bullets: quoi — what
où — where
quand — when
qui — who
comment — how
pourquoi — why
combien — how much / how many

explanation.body: Casual speech keeps statement order and tags the question word at the end. This is the spoken norm, not lazy speech.
Viens-tu ? is grammatically correct but belongs to formal writing and official speech. Hear it, understand it, move on.

examples.english: Both mean 'Are you coming?' — only the register differs.

nodes.title: Beyond pas: jamais, rien, plus, personne

explanation.body: Negation is always ne + one negative word after the verb. Pas is just the most common choice — replace it with jamais, rien, plus, or personne and nothing else changes.
bullets: jamais — never
rien — nothing / not anything
plus — no more / not anymore
personne — nobody / not anyone

explanation.body: In speech, ne disappears with all four words, not just with pas. Keeping ne marks you as formal writing, not conversation.

examples.french: J'vais jamais là-bas. / Il vient jamais. / J'ai rien dit.
examples.english: I never go there. / He never comes. / I didn't say anything.

explanation.body: When plus means 'no more', the s is silent — it sounds like plu. When plus means 'more' (plus vite), the s is heard. Pronunciation is the reliable signal.
Both words can move to the front as subjects. In writing, ne stays on the verb; in speech, it drops as usual.
French can combine jamais with personne or rien in one sentence. Both sit after the verb; one ne covers the whole thing — or none does in speech.

canonical_examples.formal: Il n'y a jamais personne ici.

nodes.title: Object pronoun order: me le, te la, le lui

explanation.body: When two object pronouns stack, the order is fixed — but there are two separate rules, not one.
bullets: me / te / nous / vous come BEFORE le / la / les
le / la / les come BEFORE lui / leur

explanation.body: First- and second-person pronouns take the front slot. Le/la/les follow them.

examples.english: Can you give it to me?

explanation.body: With third-person indirect objects, the direct object wins the front slot. Lui and leur always sit behind it.

examples.english: We have to give it to them.

explanation.header: The cluster sits before the verb
explanation.body: Both pronouns land together, immediately before the conjugated verb — or before the auxiliary in compound tenses. Le and la contract to l' before a vowel.

examples.english: He told me. (me le a → me l'a)
I told you. (te le ai → te l'ai)

explanation.body: At normal speed, vowels drop and consonants merge. Focus on recognition first; production follows with exposure.

examples.english: He's giving it to me. (il me le → il m'le)
I told you. (je te l'ai → one pulse)

nodes.title: y & en: the two little pronouns, deep dive

explanation.body: French drops a noun once the listener already knows it. Y and en are the two pronouns that cover specific grammatical slots for that job.

examples.french: Tu penses à ce problème ? — J'y pense.
examples.english: Thinking about that problem? — I'm thinking about it.

examples.french: Tu veux du café ? — J'en veux.
examples.english: Want some coffee? — I want some.

explanation.header: Y replaces à + thing or place
explanation.body: Any verb that takes à — penser à, aller à, croire à — gets y instead. Places work the same: à Paris, au bureau all become y.

explanation.header: En replaces de + thing and quantities
explanation.body: En replaces de + noun for verbs like parler de or avoir besoin de. With numbers or quantity words, the number stays after the verb — it doesn't disappear.
bullets: de + noun → en: Tu parles de ça ? — J'en parle.
number follows verb: J'en veux deux. / J'en ai plein.

explanation.body: Some common phrases are frozen around y or en. Their internal logic isn't always transparent — learn them whole.
bullets: Ça y est ! — That's it, done.
On y va ! — Let's go.
J'en peux plus. — I can't take it anymore.
T'en fais pas. — Don't worry about it.

explanation.body: Il y a shrinks to y'a; il y en a shrinks to y'en a. These are the spoken existentials — you'll hear them dozens of times a day.

examples.english: There are three of them. (Il y en a trois.)
There's none left. (Il n'y en a plus.)

nodes.title: Comparatives & superlatives: plus, moins, aussi

explanation.body: Put plus, moins, or aussi before the adjective. Use que to introduce what you're comparing to. The adjective still agrees in gender and number normally.

examples.english: It's more expensive than in Paris.

explanation.body: Never say plus bon or plus bien — both have their own forms. Meilleur is an adjective and agrees; mieux never changes.
bullets: bon → meilleur/meilleure: Ce resto est meilleur que l'autre.
bien → mieux: C'est mieux ici.

explanation.body: Add le, la, or les before plus or moins. The article matches the noun's gender and number.

examples.french: C'est carrément la solution la plus simple.

explanation.body: Plus meaning 'more' has a pronounced s. Plus meaning 'no more' in a negative has a silent s. One sentence can contain both.

examples.french: J'en veux plus — c'est plus intéressant là-bas.
examples.english: I don't want any more — it's more interesting over there.

explanation.body: Spoken French uses c'est as the default carrier for comparisons — it's shorter than a full subject-verb phrase. Carrément intensifies superlatives in casual speech.
bullets: c'est mieux — preferred over il est mieux in most spoken contexts
c'est moins cher — the go-to for price comparisons
c'est carrément plus simple — carrément adds emphasis without sounding stiff

nodes.title: Imperatives: commands without a subject

explanation.body: A command has no subject. The person you're talking to is obvious, so the verb stands alone.
In the tu-form command, -er verbs lose their final s. There's no logic to recover — just apply it every time.

examples.french: Exception: vas-y (aller keeps the s before y)

explanation.body: In a positive command, object pronouns move behind the verb with a hyphen. Me becomes moi in that position.
Wrap the verb in ne … pas. In speech, ne drops constantly — know both forms.

examples.french: Ne touche pas ça ! → Touche pas ça !

explanation.body: A bare imperative isn't rude, but s'il te plaît (informal) or s'il vous plaît (formal/plural) softens it when needed.
These short imperatives appear in almost every conversation. Recognising them fast is half the work.
bullets: Vas-y — go ahead / come on / go for it
Attends — wait / hold on
Arrête — stop it
Laisse tomber — forget it / drop it
T'inquiète — don't worry (ne and pas both gone)

nodes.title: Reflexive verbs: the daily-routine engine

explanation.body: The subject acts on itself. A small pronoun between subject and verb marks this — remove it and the meaning changes.

examples.french: lever = to raise / se lever = to get up
examples.english: The pronoun changes the meaning entirely.

explanation.body: Each pronoun matches its subject and sits directly before the verb.
bullets: je me lève — I get up
tu te lèves — you get up
il / elle / on se lève — he / she / one gets up
nous nous levons / vous vous levez — we / you get up

explanation.body: Routine verbs and social verbs cover most of what you'll say.
bullets: Routine: se lever, se réveiller, se doucher, s'habiller, se coucher
Social: se voir, s'appeler, se retrouver, se parler

examples.french: On va se retrouver au café.
examples.english: We're going to meet up at the café.

explanation.body: With on or a plural subject, the construction means 'each other'. Context makes the direction clear.
Reflexive verbs take être, not avoir. The past participle agrees with the subject: add -e for feminine, -s for plural.

examples.french: J'me suis levé tard ce matin.
examples.english: I got up late this morning. (male speaker)

examples.french: On s'est vus hier — ça faisait longtemps.
examples.english: We saw each other yesterday — it had been a long time. (-s because plural)

nodes.title: Partitives: du, de la, des — 'some'

explanation.body: English drops 'some' freely. French can't — skipping the partitive breaks the sentence.

examples.french: J'veux du pain. / Y'a du café et des frites.
examples.english: I want (some) bread. / There's (some) coffee and (some) fries.

explanation.body: The partitive is de + the definite article. Gender and number drive the choice.
bullets: du (de + le) — masculine: du pain, du café
de la — feminine: de la bière
de l' — before a vowel, either gender: de l'eau
des — plural: des frites, des trucs

explanation.body: After any negative, all four forms shrink to plain de. Negation cancels the quantity, so the quantity marker disappears.

examples.french: J'ai pas de pain. / Y'a plus d'lait.
examples.english: I don't have any bread. / There's no more milk.

explanation.body: Beaucoup de, un peu de, trop de — all drop the article. The quantity word already carries the job.

examples.french: Y'a beaucoup d'monde et trop d'trucs à faire.
examples.english: There are a lot of people and too many things to do.

explanation.body: Je prends and je voudrais take a partitive directly. These two frames cover most real-life use.

examples.french: On va prendre d'la bière au café — on a pas beaucoup d'temps.
examples.english: We're going to have some beer at the café — we haven't got much time.

explanation.header: Street French: de compresses, never drops
explanation.body: Fast speech never removes the partitive — that's a grammar error, not a style choice. Before a vowel, de glues into the noun: d'lait, d'eau, d'monde.

canonical_examples.formal: Je voudrais de l'eau, s'il vous plaît.

nodes.title: il faut & impersonal expressions

explanation.body: Il faut has no real subject — il is like 'it' in 'it's raining'. One fixed form covers everyone: you, me, all of us.

examples.english: You have to leave. / We have to leave.

explanation.body: Faut takes an infinitive for obligation or a noun for what's needed.
bullets: faut + infinitive → obligation: il faut voir ça (you have to see that)
faut + noun → necessity: il faut une réservation (we need a reservation)

explanation.header: Faut vs. tu dois for advice
explanation.body: Tu dois points at one person and feels heavy. Faut is impersonal, so it lands like general wisdom.

examples.english: You shouldn't do that. (gentler than tu ne dois pas)

explanation.body: Faut isn't alone. These expressions work the same way: fixed form, no subject to pick.
bullets: il vaut mieux + infinitive: il vaut mieux attendre (it's better to wait)
ça prend: ça prend du temps (it takes time)
il reste: il reste du pain (there's some bread left)

explanation.body: Swap faut for fallait to go into the past. Nothing else changes. It covers 'had to' and 'should have'.
In spoken French, il disappears entirely. This is the norm, not laziness. Keep il in writing; drop it in speech.
bullets: il faut partir → faut partir
il ne faut pas faire ça → faut pas faire ça (ne drops too)
il vaut mieux attendre → vaut mieux attendre

nodes.title: Time & date expressions

explanation.body: The frame is always il est + number + heures. Three fixed phrases handle the quarters.
bullets: et quart — quarter past: il est neuf heures et quart
et demie — half past: il est trois heures et demie
moins le quart — quarter to: il est huit heures moins le quart

examples.french: Il est huit heures moins le quart — j'dois partir.
examples.english: It's quarter to eight — I have to leave.

explanation.body: Official schedules always use 24-hour time. You won't need it in casual talk, but you'll see it constantly. Subtract 12 from any hour above 12.

examples.french: La réunion est à quatorze heures trente.
examples.english: The meeting is at two thirty p.m.

explanation.header: Days and months: lowercase, no article
explanation.body: French never capitalises days or months. You also drop the article — lundi alone means 'on Monday'. Le lundi shifts the meaning to 'every Monday'.
bullets: Days: lundi, mardi, mercredi, jeudi, vendredi, samedi, dimanche
Months with en: en janvier, en mars, en octobre — all lowercase

explanation.body: These seven words cover almost every time reference in real conversation. Learn them as a fixed set.
bullets: aujourd'hui — today
demain — tomorrow
hier — yesterday
ce matin / ce soir — this morning / this evening
la semaine prochaine — next week

explanation.header: À pins a time to an event
explanation.body: Use à before any clock time or anchor word for appointments. À demain is a complete sentence — the à implies 'until we meet at that time'.
English uses the past tense for 'I've lived here for two years'. French sees it as present and uses depuis with the present tense. Past tense + depuis signals the action has stopped.

examples.english: I've been living here for two years (and still do).

explanation.body: Spoken time talk compresses into fixed chunks. These aren't sloppy — they're the normal register.
bullets: à toute — see you soon (à toute à l'heure, shortened)
à lundi — see you Monday
c'est quand ? — when is it? (default for any event)

nodes.title: Pointing & owning: ce, cette, mon, ton, son

explanation.header: Ce / cette / ces: this or that
explanation.body: French uses the same word for both 'this' and 'that' — context handles distance. The form tracks the noun's gender and number only.
bullets: ce + masculine singular: ce mec, ce truc
cette + feminine singular: cette table, cette meuf
ces + any plural: ces gens, ces trucs
cet + masculine singular before a vowel: cet après-midi, cet homme

explanation.body: Tack -là onto the noun to mean 'that one right there' or to contrast two things. It works at every register.

examples.english: I want that thing right there.

examples.french: Cette table-là — ton café, il est dessus.
examples.english: That table there — your coffee is on it.

explanation.header: Possessives track the noun, not the owner
explanation.body: This trips most learners. Mon / ton / son agrees with the thing owned, not with who owns it.

examples.english: His / her car — that's impossible. (sa = feminine because voiture is feminine)

explanation.body: Singular splits by gender; plural doesn't. Notre / votre / leur follow the same logic — pick them up as you hear them.
bullets: mon / ma / mes — my
ton / ta / tes — your (informal)
son / sa / ses — his, her, its

explanation.body: Ma becomes mon before a feminine noun starting with a vowel. The noun stays feminine; only the possessive changes to avoid a vowel clash.

examples.french: Mon amie — elle lui a parlé.
examples.english: My friend — she spoke to him. (amie is feminine; mon replaces ma)

explanation.body: Demonstratives carry casual speech. Ce mec, cette meuf, ce truc-là appear constantly in real conversation.

examples.english: These people, I don't know them.

nodes.title: c'est vs il est

explanation.body: C'est identifies — it names or labels something. Il/elle est describes — it adds a quality to something already named.
Use c'est whenever you're naming or pointing at something for the first time. It takes a noun, a name, or a stressed pronoun.
bullets: noun: C'est mon frère
name: C'est Marie
stressed pronoun: C'est moi, c'est lui

explanation.body: Once a person or thing is already named, attach a bare adjective with il est or elle est. The adjective agrees with their gender.
When you're commenting on a situation — not a named person — use c'est, even with an adjective. There's no specific he or she; c'est points at the whole thing.
No article means the job is a category — use elle/il est. Add an article and it becomes a noun phrase — use c'est.
bullets: bare: Elle est médecin
with article: C'est une médecin

explanation.body: In speech, c'est handles questions, verdicts, and negation. Ne drops completely — c'est pas is standard.
bullets: identity question: C'est qui ?
what-is-this: C'est quoi ça ?
verdict: C'est mort
negation: C'est pas possible

canonical_examples.formal: C'est une médecin. / Elle est médecin.
canonical_examples.street: C'est une médecin. / Elle est médecin.

nodes.title: Just did / doing right now: venir de & être en train de

explanation.body: French uses a frame + infinitive instead of a special tense. Only the first verb conjugates — the infinitive after de never changes.

examples.french: Je viens de manger. / Je suis en train de manger.
examples.english: I just ate. / I'm in the middle of eating.

explanation.body: Venir de + infinitive places an action right before now. English hides this in tense; French makes it explicit.

examples.french: Je viens de manger. — Il vient de partir.
examples.english: I just ate. — He just left.

explanation.header: Être en train de: right now
explanation.body: Être en train de + infinitive means you're in the middle of something. Use it to flag active, ongoing action.

examples.french: Je suis en train de travailler. — On est en train de manger — on te rappelle.
examples.english: I'm in the middle of working. — We're in the middle of eating — we'll call you back.

explanation.body: Put venir or être into the imparfait for background past equivalents. This follows the same imparfait logic you already know.
bullets: venais de + infinitive → had just: elle venait d'arriver quand il a commencé à pleuvoir
étais en train de + infinitive → was in the middle of: j'étais en train de payer quand le système est tombé en panne

explanation.body: These two frames plus futur proche cover every position around a moment.
bullets: je viens de manger — just did (past edge)
je suis en train de manger — doing right now
je vais manger — about to (futur proche)

examples.english: I just did it — I'm about to leave.

explanation.body: Fast speech shortens the frame but never removes it — without it, the meaning disappears.
bullets: je → j' : j'viens de manger
je suis / tu es → chuis / j'suis : chuis en train de manger
de before consonant → d' : il vient d'partir

nodes.title: Futur simple: when it beats futur proche

explanation.body: Take the infinitive and add -ai, -as, -a, -ez, -ont. For -re verbs, drop the final e first.
bullets: parler → je parlerai, tu parleras, il parlera
finir → je finirai, tu finiras, il finira
prendre → prendr- → je prendrai, tu prendras, il prendra

explanation.body: The stem changes; the endings stay identical. These eight cover nearly every irregular you'll hear.
bullets: être → ser- (je serai)
avoir → aur- (t'auras)
aller → ir- (j'irai)
faire → fer- (on fera)
pouvoir → pourr- (tu pourras)
vouloir → voudr- (il voudra)
falloir → faudr- (il faudra)
venir → viendr- (tu viendras)

explanation.body: Futur proche is the spoken default for plans. Futur simple adds distance or weight — use it for promises and uncertain futures.

examples.english: I'm heading back tomorrow. → futur proche
I'll call you tonight. → futur simple

explanation.body: After quand when the event is in the future, use futur simple in both clauses — present tense is not allowed there.

explanation.body: Spoken French leans on futur proche for everyday plans. Futur simple survives in a handful of fixed phrases — learn these whole.
bullets: on verra — we'll see
ça ira — it'll be fine

nodes.title: Survival subjunctive: il faut que + the forms you'll hear

explanation.body: After certain triggers, French switches the next verb to signal wanting, requiring, or doubting — not stating fact.

explanation.header: Il faut que: the one trigger to own
explanation.body: Il faut que + subjunctive handles obligation. In conversation it largely replaces devoir — treat it as a frame you drop verbs into.
Most verbs sound identical to the present you already know. Only these eight common verbs have audibly distinct subjunctive forms — learn them, and you're covered.
bullets: être → sois / soit — faut que tu sois là
avoir → aies / ait — faut qu'elle ait le temps
aller → aille — faut que j'aille
faire → fasse — faut qu'on fasse ça
pouvoir → puisse — pour que tu puisses venir
savoir → sache — faut que tu saches
venir → vienne — faut qu'elle vienne
prendre → prenne — faut que je prenne le train

explanation.body: If a verb isn't on that list, its subjunctive sounds like the present. You're already saying it correctly.

examples.english: You need to eat. (sounds like the present)

explanation.header: Street French: how it actually sounds
explanation.body: Il drops to faut que, and que elides to qu' before a vowel. The whole structure collapses into one fast chunk.

nodes.title: Si clauses: real ifs and imagined ifs

explanation.body: The tense after si tells you everything. Présent = real or likely. Imparfait = imagined, not true right now.

examples.french: Si t'es libre, on peut manger ici.
examples.english: If you're free, we can eat here. (real)

examples.french: S'il faisait beau, on irait à la plage.
examples.english: If it was nice out, we'd go to the beach. (imagined)

explanation.header: Real si: présent → présent or futur
explanation.body: Use si + présent for anything that could actually happen. The result clause uses présent or futur proche — both work.

examples.french: Si y'a un café, on y va.
examples.english: If there's a café, we'll go.

explanation.body: Use si + imparfait for things that aren't true now. The result clause uses the conditionnel.

examples.french: Si j'avais l'argent, j'voudrais aller à Paris.
examples.english: If I had the money, I'd want to go to Paris.

explanation.header: Never futur or conditionnel after si
explanation.body: This is the rule French children memorise. Futur and conditionnel only appear in the result clause, never after si.
bullets: ✗ Si je viendrai… — impossible
✗ Si j'aurais le temps… — impossible
✓ Si je viens… / Si j'avais le temps, je viendrais.

explanation.body: Si on followed by imparfait, said with rising intonation, is how you propose plans. There's no result clause — the si clause does it all.

examples.english: How about we go to the restaurant?

explanation.body: Si elides to s' only before il and ils. Before elle, on, or anything else, si stays si — no elision.
bullets: s'il te plaît, s'il pleut — elision before il ✓
si elle appelle, si on y va — no elision ✓
Street: si t'as → si j'peux → si y'a — same compression rules as all spoken French

nodes.title: Relatives II: dont & où

explanation.body: Qui is the subject of its clause. Que is the object. Dont and où fill two more slots.

examples.french: Le mec qui parle / le mec que je connais.
examples.english: The guy who's talking / the guy I know.

explanation.body: Où covers both 'where' and 'when' in relative clauses. English switches to 'when' for time; French stays with où.

examples.english: The café we go to is nice.

examples.french: Le jour où j'suis arrivé, il faisait beau.

explanation.header: Dont: whenever the verb takes de
explanation.body: Dont replaces de + a noun. The verb inside the relative clause tells you to use it.
bullets: parler de → le mec dont j't'ai parlé (the guy I told you about)
avoir besoin de → ce dont j'ai besoin (what I need)
se souvenir de → le film dont j'me souviens (the film I remember)

explanation.body: Look at the verb in the relative clause. Ask what preposition it normally takes. That decides the pronoun.
bullets: Subject → qui
Object, no preposition → que
Verb takes de → dont
Place or time → où

explanation.body: Unlike ne-drop or on-for-nous, relatives don't simplify in speech. They're everywhere in storytelling — recognising them matters as much as producing them.

nodes.title: Verb + preposition patterns: essayer de, commencer à

explanation.body: When verbs chain, only the first one conjugates. The second stays as a bare infinitive. Which preposition sits between them depends entirely on the first verb — learn each verb with its preposition as one unit.

examples.english: I want to eat. (vouloir: no preposition)
I'm starting to understand. (commencer: needs à)
I'm trying to sleep. (essayer: needs de)

explanation.body: These verbs take the infinitive with no preposition at all.
bullets: vouloir, pouvoir, devoir, aller, aimer

explanation.body: These verbs need à before the infinitive. They all express effort or movement toward a result.
bullets: commencer à, apprendre à, réussir à, arriver à

explanation.body: These verbs need de before the infinitive. They treat the second action as a separate object — something tried, stopped, or avoided.
bullets: essayer de, arrêter de, décider de, oublier de, venir de, éviter de

explanation.body: Before doing something, use avant de + bare infinitive. The second verb never conjugates, whoever the subject is.
À stays as-is. De elides to d' before a vowel — same rule as always. Neither preposition disappears; they just compress.

nodes.title: Two pronouns at once: je te le dis

explanation.body: French lets you replace both objects at once. The pronouns stack before the verb in five slots, always left to right.
bullets: slot 1 — me / te / nous / vous
slot 2 — le / la / les
slot 3 — lui / leur
slot 4 — y
slot 5 — en

explanation.header: Five pairs do all the work
explanation.body: You only ever fill two slots at once. These five pairs cover almost everything you'll say or hear.
bullets: me + le → me le : il me le donne (he gives it to me)
te + le → te le : je te le dis (I'm telling you)
le + lui → le lui : je le lui explique (I explain it to him)
me + en → m'en : donne-m'en (give me some)
y + en → y en : y en a (there's some)

explanation.header: Y en a — learn this first
explanation.body: Il y en a (there is/are some) is the single most common double-pronoun form in French. Learn it as a chunk.

explanation.header: Passé composé: pronouns before the auxiliary
explanation.body: In compound tenses, both pronouns go between the subject and the auxiliary avoir or être. The past participle follows after.

explanation.header: Street: pronouns fuse into one block
explanation.body: In fast speech, elision welds the pronouns and verb into a single sound block. Catch any part of it and you know where the verb is.

nodes.title: Degree words: trop, grave, carrément

explanation.body: Four words scale adjectives in standard French. They all sit directly before the adjective — position never moves.
bullets: un peu — a little: un peu fatigué
assez — fairly: assez bien
très — very: très bien
vraiment — really: vraiment bien

explanation.body: In formal French, trop means 'too much'. In casual speech, under-40s use it exactly where textbooks say très — the excess meaning fades, the intensity stays.

examples.french: C'est très bien. → C'est trop bien.

explanation.body: These replace très and vraiment in real spoken French. Same rule: degree word directly before the adjective.
bullets: grave — seriously: elle est grave fatiguée
carrément — completely: c'est carrément pas possible
vachement — really: c'est vachement tard
hyper / super — really: j'suis hyper content, c'est super cool

explanation.header: grave and carrément as full answers
explanation.body: Both words work as one-word replies to a yes/no question. No adjective needed — the word carries 'absolutely' on its own.
Every word on both ladders lands immediately before what it scales. There are no exceptions.

nodes.title: Discourse glue: du coup, donc, alors, bref

explanation.body: Du coup means 'so'. It chains any two thoughts, and it fills half the gaps in real speech.

examples.french: J'étais fatigué, du coup j'suis rentré.
examples.english: I was tired, so I went home.

explanation.body: Both mean 'so'. Donc draws a conclusion; alors pushes the story forward, or buys time as alors…
Parce que is 'because' — everyone says it passke. For contrast you have three strengths:
bullets: mais — but (blunt): mais c'est cher
par contre — on the other hand (softer): par contre, c'est loin
même si — even if (concedes): même si t'as raison

explanation.body: D'abord starts, après and ensuite continue, bref lands it. Bref cuts the rest: 'long story short'.
En fait means 'actually'. It opens a correction of what was just said or assumed.

explanation.header: This is what fluency sounds like
explanation.body: Connectors buy thinking time. A fluent hesitation is du coup… or alors…, not silence.

canonical_examples.formal: En fait, ce n'est pas possible.
